solution:
Coupling reaction between benzene diazonium chlorides with aniline in acidic medium takes place to give yellow colour. In this reaction the nitrogen N2 in the diazonium ion is lost and forms the bridge between two benzene rings forming an electrophilic substitution reaction.

Fluoro benzene is prepared by treating benzene diazonium chloride with fluoro boric acid. This reaction produces diazonium fluoroborate which on heating produces fluorobenzene. This reaction is called Balz-Schiemann reaction.

Gattermann reaction is used for obtaining chlorobenzene from benzenediazonium chloride by treating it with Cu/HCl  respectively.

Similarly, Benzene diazonium chloride solution on reaction with cuprous cyanide and potassium cyanide gives benzonitrile. This reaction is known as Sandmeyer's reaction.

q: A compound with molecular mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn></math> is acylated with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COCl</mi></math> to get a compound with molecular mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>390</mn></math>. The number of amino groups presents per molecule of the former compound is
question: A compound with molecular mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn></math> is acylated with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COCl</mi></math> to get a compound with molecular mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>390</mn></math>. The number of amino groups presents per molecule of the former compound is
solution:
After acylation the formula will be R-(NHCOCH3)x. Hence, the change in the molecular mass is calculated as below

For 1one NH2→14+2=16 unitsWhen It turns into NH−COCH3  ​  →14+1+12+16+12+3=58 Change in mass⇒58−16=42units. So for every NH2 , there will be an increase of 42g after acylation.  ⇒ The total increase in mass = 390−180 = 210  ⇒ No. of amino groups=21042= 5 Hence number of amino groups are 5.

(12+16+12+3-1)x=390-180

or 42x=210; or x=5

subject: Chemistry
q:
Compound ' P ' undergoes the following sequence of reactions :

' $\mathrm{P}^{\prime}$ is :
question:
Compound ' P ' undergoes the following sequence of reactions :

' $\mathrm{P}^{\prime}$ is :
solution:
The final product is cyclohexyl isocyanide ($C_6H_{11}NC$).
Working backwards from the final product:
The last step involves $CHCl_3$ and $KOH$ (alc), which is the Carbylamine reaction.
This reaction converts a primary amine ($R-NH_2$) into an isocyanide ($R-NC$). Thus, the intermediate before this step must be cyclohexylamine ($C_6H_{11}NH_2$).
The step before cyclohexylamine involves $Br_2$ and $KOH$, which is the Hoffmann Bromamide Degradation reaction.
This reaction converts an amide ($R-CONH_2$) into a primary amine ($R-NH_2$) with one less carbon atom.
Therefore, the amide 'Q' must be cyclohexanecarboxamide ($C_6H_{11}CONH_2$).
The first step converts 'P' into 'Q' using $NH_3$ and heat.
Carboxylic acids react with $NH_3$ followed by heating to form amides.
Thus, 'P' must be cyclohexanecarboxylic acid ($C_6H_{11}COOH$).
Reaction sequence:
$C_6H_{11}COOH$ (P) $\xrightarrow{NH_3, \Delta} C_6H_{11}CONH_2$ (Q)
$C_6H_{11}CONH_2$ (Q) $\xrightarrow{Br_2, KOH} C_6H_{11}NH_2$ (Cyclohexylamine)
$C_6H_{11}NH_2 \xrightarrow{CHCl_3, KOH} C_6H_{11}NC$ (Cyclohexyl isocyanide)
Comparing with the options, 'P' is cyclohexanecarboxylic acid, which corresponds to option (4).

subject: Chemistry
q: The correct order in aqueous medium of basic strength in case of methyl substituted amines is:
question: The correct order in aqueous medium of basic strength in case of methyl substituted amines is:
solution:
In aqueous medium basic strength is dependent on electron density on nitrogen as well as solvation of cation formed after accepting H+. In protic solvent like water, the order of basic strength is

Me2NH>MeNH2>Me3N>NH3.

This happens beacuse in case of Me2NH there will be optimum (+I) and solvation while in MeNH2 has more significant hydration than Me3N.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following sequence of reactions.

Assuming that the reaction proceeds to completion, then 137 mg of 4 -nitrotoluene will produce $\_\_\_\_$ mg of $B$. (Given molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{C}: 12, \mathrm{~N}: 14, \mathrm{O}: 16, \mathrm{Br}: 80$)
question:
Consider the following sequence of reactions.

Assuming that the reaction proceeds to completion, then 137 mg of 4 -nitrotoluene will produce $\_\_\_\_$ mg of $B$. (Given molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{C}: 12, \mathrm{~N}: 14, \mathrm{O}: 16, \mathrm{Br}: 80$)
solution:
Starting with 137 mg of 4-nitrotoluene (C₇H₉NO₂, MW = 137).
Step 1: Reduction with Sn/HCl converts NO₂ to NH₂, producing 4-aminotoluene (A).
Step 2: Acetylation with (CH₃CO)₂O produces the acetamide intermediate, then bromination with Br₂/AcOH adds bromine to the para position (ortho-para directing due to acetamide group), yielding 4-bromo-N-(4-methylphenyl)acetamide (B) with molecular formula C₉H₁₀BrNO and MW = 228 g/mol.
Moles of starting material = 137 mg ÷ 137 g/mol = 1 mmol.
Assuming complete reaction, moles of B produced = 1 mmol.
Mass of B = 1 mmol × 228 mg/mmol = 228 mg.

subject: Chemistry
q: An organic compound $(\mathrm{P})$ on treatment with aqueous ammonia under hot condition forms compound (Q) which on heating with $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ and KOH forms compound (R) having molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{7} \mathrm{~N}$. Names of P, Q and R respectively are.
question: An organic compound $(\mathrm{P})$ on treatment with aqueous ammonia under hot condition forms compound (Q) which on heating with $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ and KOH forms compound (R) having molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{7} \mathrm{~N}$. Names of P, Q and R respectively are.
solution:
Q + $Br_2$/KOH → R is Hofmann bromamide degradation: Amide → Amine (with one less carbon).
R = $C_6H_7N$ = $C_6H_5NH_2$ = Aniline
For Q to give aniline, Q = Benzamide ($C_6H_5CONH_2$)
$C_6H_5CONH_2 \xrightarrow{Br_2/KOH} C_6H_5NH_2$
P + hot $NH_3$ → Q: Carboxylic acid + $NH_3$ → Amide
P = Benzoic acid ($C_6H_5COOH$)
$C_6H_5COOH \xrightarrow{NH_3, \Delta} C_6H_5CONH_2$
P = Benzoic acid, Q = Benzamide, R = Aniline

subject: Chemistry
q:
Total number of alkali insoluble solid sulphonamides obtained by reaction of given amines with Hinsberg's reagent is $\_\_\_\_$.
Aniline, N-Methylaniline, Methanamine, N, N-Dimethylmethanamine, N-Methyl methanamine, Phenylmethanamine, N-propylaniline, N-phenylaniline, N, N-Dimethylaniline, Allyl amine, Isopropyl amine
question:
Total number of alkali insoluble solid sulphonamides obtained by reaction of given amines with Hinsberg's reagent is $\_\_\_\_$.
Aniline, N-Methylaniline, Methanamine, N, N-Dimethylmethanamine, N-Methyl methanamine, Phenylmethanamine, N-propylaniline, N-phenylaniline, N, N-Dimethylaniline, Allyl amine, Isopropyl amine
solution:
Hinsberg's reagent (benzenesulfonyl chloride) classifies amines by their reaction products:
Primary amines (RNH₂) form alkali-soluble sulfonamides (soluble in NaOH).
Secondary amines (R₂NH) form alkali-insoluble sulfonamides (precipitate in NaOH).
Tertiary amines (R₃N) do not react.
Analyzing the given amines:
Aniline (primary), Methanamine (primary), Phenylmethanamine (primary), Allyl amine (primary), Isopropyl amine (primary) → alkali-soluble.
N,N-Dimethylmethanamine (tertiary), N,N-Dimethylaniline (tertiary) → no reaction.
N-Methylaniline (secondary), N-Methyl methanamine (secondary), N-Propylaniline (secondary), N-Phenylaniline (secondary) → alkali-insoluble.
Total alkali-insoluble sulfonamides: 4.

subject: Chemistry
q: The mass of benzanilide obtained from the benzoylation reaction of 5.8 g of aniline, if yield of product is $82 \%$, is $\_\_\_\_$ g (nearest integer). (Given molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{C}: 12, \mathrm{~N}: 14, \mathrm{O}: 16$)
question: The mass of benzanilide obtained from the benzoylation reaction of 5.8 g of aniline, if yield of product is $82 \%$, is $\_\_\_\_$ g (nearest integer). (Given molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{C}: 12, \mathrm{~N}: 14, \mathrm{O}: 16$)
solution:
Benzoylation of aniline:
$C_6H_5NH_2 + C_6H_5COCl \rightarrow C_6H_5CONHC_6H_5 + HCl$

Molar mass of aniline:
$M = 6(12) + 7(1) + 14 = 93$ g/mol

Moles of aniline:
$n = \frac{5.8}{93} = 0.0624$ mol

Molar mass of benzanilide ($C_{13}H_{11}NO$):
$M = 13(12) + 11(1) + 14 + 16 = 197$ g/mol

Theoretical mass of benzanilide:
$m_{theoretical} = 0.0624 \times 197 = 12.29$ g

Actual mass with 82% yield:
$m_{actual} = 12.29 \times 0.82 = 10.08 \approx 10$ g

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: Heating benzamide with bromine in an ethanolic solution of sodium hydroxide will give benzylamine.
Statement II: Nitration of aniline with $HNO_3/H_2SO_4$ at $288\text{ K}$ produces $m$-nitroaniline in higher amount than $o$-nitroaniline (pH adjusted).
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: Heating benzamide with bromine in an ethanolic solution of sodium hydroxide will give benzylamine.
Statement II: Nitration of aniline with $HNO_3/H_2SO_4$ at $288\text{ K}$ produces $m$-nitroaniline in higher amount than $o$-nitroaniline (pH adjusted).
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
Statement I describes the Hoffmann bromamide degradation reaction. When benzamide ($C_6H_5CONH_2$) is heated with bromine in an ethanolic solution of sodium hydroxide, it produces aniline ($C_6H_5NH_2$), not benzylamine. Thus, Statement I is false.

Statement II describes the nitration of aniline. Direct nitration of aniline with $HNO_3/H_2SO_4$ yields a mixture of $p$-nitroaniline ($51\%$), $m$-nitroaniline ($47\%$), and $o$-nitroaniline ($2\%$). The significant formation of the meta derivative is due to the protonation of aniline in the strongly acidic medium to form the anilinium ion, which is meta-directing. Since $47\% > 2\%$, $m$-nitroaniline is produced in a higher amount than $o$-nitroaniline. Thus, Statement II is true.

Answer: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
